legalFormLocal: Private company limited by shares (חברה פרטית)
summary: The Registrar of Companies keeps a register for every company and the registers are open to public inspection with certified copies obtainable (s.38, s.43). Any person can obtain a full company extract (נסח חברה) for a fee; for a private company it shows current registered particulars including status, registered office, directors/officers and shareholders with their holdings. This extract is the current register extract and the base evidentiary document.

evidence.quote: The registers kept by the Registrar in the registers office shall be open to inspection by the public, and every person may inspect them and receive certified copies of their contents, whether through the Registrar or through others whom the Registrar authorized for this purpose, all as the Minister prescribed.
evidence.note: s.38 requires the Registrar to keep registers for each company and a Register of Companies; s.41 makes a Registrar-certified copy conclusive proof.

evidence.basis: Israeli Corporations Authority / Registrar of Companies — company extract service (gov.il / ica.justice.gov.il)
evidence.quote: This service allows any person to obtain basic information free of charge on companies and partnerships, or a full extract for a fee.
evidence.note: Wording per the gov.il 'company extract' service page as surfaced in search; gov.il blocks automated fetch so exact wording not independently re-fetched. Companion 'reverse search' service states it 'provides details on office holders and shareholders, including tenure dates and shareholding periods.'

caveats: The full (paid) extract, not the free basic lookup, carries the detailed particulars.
INFERENCE that the extract heading = REGISTERAUSZUG equivalent; grounded in s.38/s.43 register-inspection rights plus the gov.il service description.

legalFormLocal: Private company limited by shares (חברה פרטית)
summary: Current shareholders of a private company are provable from the Registrar. The company itself keeps the statutory Shareholders Register (s.127/s.130), but a private company must file with the Registrar every share allocation within 14 days (s.140(5), s.292), every share transfer within 14 days (s.140(6), s.299), and an annual report (s.140, s.141) whose prescribed form lists the shareholders and their holdings. The company extract (נסח חברה) therefore reflects the current shareholders and is the base document evidencing them.

evidence.basis: Companies Law 5759-1999 (Israel) s.140 (Reports of a private company)
evidence.quote: A private company shall send the Registrar an annual report, as said in section 141, and it shall also report to the Registrar as specified in this Law and on the following matters: ... (5) share allocations, as said in section 292; (6) share transfers, as said in section 299, within 14 days after the transfer;
evidence.note: Establishes that allotments and transfers of a private company are Registrar-filed, so the current shareholder position is provable from registry filings.

evidence.basis: Companies Law 5759-1999 (Israel) s.292 (Report of allocation) and s.141 (Annual report)
evidence.quote: 292. A private company must deliver the following documents to the Registrar within 14 days after the allocation of shares: (1) a report on a form to be prescribed by the Minister, specifying the particulars of the allocation;
evidence.note: s.141: 'Once a year a private company shall draw up and submit to the Registrar an annual report ... within 14 days after the General Meeting.' The prescribed annual-report form (Companies Regulations (Annual Report and Registration) 5760-2000) itemises shareholders and holdings.

evidence.quote: 127. A company shall keep a Shareholders Register.
evidence.note: s.130 lists register contents (name, ID, address, number/category of shares, dates of allocation/transfer). This statutory register is kept by the COMPANY, not the Registrar; the Registrar's extract is derived from the s.292/s.299/s.141 filings.

caveats: Currency depends on the company having filed its 14-day allotment/transfer notices and annual report; the definitive statutory Shareholders Register is company-held (s.127), the extract is the Registrar-derived copy.
A shareholder may be a trustee (s.131) — registered holder is not necessarily beneficial owner.

legalFormLocal: Private company limited by shares (חברה פרטית)
summary: Every company must have articles (תקנון; termed 'by-laws' in the official English translation), and a copy is filed with the Registrar as an attachment to the incorporation application (s.8(1), s.15). Any change of the articles must be delivered to the Registrar (s.21(b), reported under s.140(1)). The current articles are therefore obtainable from the company's file at the Registrar as a separate document (not part of the summary extract), under the public-inspection right in s.43.

evidence.basis: Companies Law 5759-1999 (Israel) s.8 (Application for registration) & s.15
evidence.quote: 8. If a person wishes to register a company, then he shall submit to the Registrar an application on a form prescribed by the Minister, and he shall attach to it: (1) a copy of the by-laws;
evidence.note: s.15: 'Every company shall have by-laws, as specified in this Article.' 'By-laws' is the translation of תקנון (articles of association / constitution).

evidence.basis: Companies Law 5759-1999 (Israel) s.21 & s.140(1) (change of by-laws reported to Registrar)
evidence.quote: If a company adopted a resolution on a change of its by-laws, then it shall deliver the text of the decision to the Registrar within ...
evidence.note: s.140(1) lists 'changes in the by-laws, as said in section 21' among the private company's mandatory Registrar reports, so the amended articles reach the Registrar's file.

caveats: The full articles text is retrieved from the company's document file at the Registrar, not from the short company extract; ordering the underlying filed document may be a separate request.
Currency depends on the company having filed its s.21/s.140(1) amendment; a company incorporated before the 1999 Law may rely on transitional memorandum/articles (s.24).

summary: A public company is registered in the Register of Companies and a company extract (נסח חברה) is obtainable for it, evidencing legal existence, registration number, name, registered office and status. However, under s.145 a public company reports to the Registrar only on name change, registered-office change, merger and conversion — director changes (s.223 applies only to private companies) and shareholdings are NOT filed with the Registrar — so the public-company extract is materially thinner than a private one.

evidence.basis: Companies Law 5759-1999 (Israel) s.145 (Public company's report to the Registrar)
evidence.quote: Without derogating from the provisions of any statute, a public company shall report to the Registrar under this Law only on the matters specified below: (1) a decision to change the name, as said in section 31; (2) a change in the address of the registered office, as said in section 123; (3) a merger, as said in section 317; (4) its conversion into a public company, as said in section 343.
evidence.note: Confirms the extract's registry-sourced content for a public company is limited to identity/status matters; directors and shareholders are reported elsewhere (Securities Authority / TASE).

evidence.basis: Israeli Corporations Authority / Registrar of Companies — company extract service (gov.il / ica.justice.gov.il)
evidence.quote: This service allows any person to obtain basic information free of charge on companies and partnerships, or a full extract for a fee.
evidence.note: Same extract service applies to public companies; wording per gov.il service page as surfaced in search (gov.il blocks automated fetch).

caveats: Public-company extract carries limited data: per s.145 (and s.223 being private-company-only) directors and shareholders are not filed with the Registrar.
INFERENCE that the same נסח חברה product exists for public companies; grounded in the single Register of Companies (s.38) and universal extract service.

summary: Current shareholders of a public/listed company are NOT provable from the Registrar of Companies. A public company does not report allotments, transfers or shareholders to the Registrar (s.145 exhaustively lists its Registrar reports, excluding these). Listed shares are held on the TASE through a registration company (nominee) which is expressly not deemed a shareholder (s.132, s.177(1)). Only substantial (5%+) holdings are disclosed — to the Securities Authority under the Securities Law, recorded in the company-held Register of Substantial Shareholders (s.128, s.137) — not a complete current shareholder list at the Registrar.

evidence.basis: Companies Law 5759-1999 (Israel) s.145 (Public company's report to the Registrar)
evidence.quote: a public company shall report to the Registrar under this Law only on the matters specified below: (1) a decision to change the name ...; (2) a change in the address of the registered office ...; (3) a merger ...; (4) its conversion into a public company ...
evidence.note: Share allocations (s.292) and transfers (s.299) — mandatory for private companies under s.140 — are absent, so shareholder data does not reach the Registrar for public companies.

evidence.quote: a registration company shall not be deemed a shareholder in the company and the shares in its name are owned by the persons entitled to them, as said in section 177(1).
evidence.note: Listed shares sit in the name of a nominee/registration company; the registered holder is not the beneficial owner, so the register does not reveal current beneficial shareholders.

evidence.basis: Companies Law 5759-1999 (Israel) s.128 & s.137 (Register of Substantial Shareholders)
evidence.quote: 137. In the Register of Substantial Shareholders shall be kept all the reports received by the company under the Securities Law on the holdings of company shares by substantial shareholders.
evidence.note: Only 5%+ ('substantial') holders are disclosed, via Securities Law reporting to the Israel Securities Authority (MAGNA), and this register is company-held — not the Registrar of Companies. Not a full shareholder list.

caveats: Substantial (5%+) shareholders ARE discoverable via the Israel Securities Authority / MAGNA and the company's Register of Substantial Shareholders — but that is not the Registrar of Companies and not a complete current shareholder list.
Listed-company ordinary shares are held through TASE nominee / registration company (s.132), masking beneficial owners.
documentLocal set to the extract for schema consistency, but the extract does not evidence current public-company shareholders.

summary: A public company must have articles (תקנון) and a copy was filed with the Registrar on incorporation (s.8(1), s.15), obtainable from its Registrar file under the s.43 inspection right. However, s.145 does NOT include a change of by-laws among a public company's mandatory Registrar reports, so amendments to the articles of a public/listed company are filed with the Israel Securities Authority (MAGNA / prospectus regime) rather than the Registrar. The Registrar's file therefore proves the constitution as a filed document but may hold only the founding (or pre-listing) version.

evidence.basis: Companies Law 5759-1999 (Israel) s.8(1) & s.15 (articles filed at incorporation)
evidence.quote: 8. ... he shall attach to it: (1) a copy of the by-laws;
evidence.note: s.15: 'Every company shall have by-laws.' The founding articles of every company, public included, are on the Registrar's incorporation file.

evidence.basis: Companies Law 5759-1999 (Israel) s.145 vs s.140(1) (amendment reporting)
evidence.quote: a public company shall report to the Registrar under this Law only on the matters specified below: (1) a decision to change the name ...; (2) ... registered office ...; (3) a merger ...; (4) its conversion into a public company ...
evidence.note: Change of by-laws (s.21) — a mandatory private-company Registrar report under s.140(1) — is absent from the public-company list in s.145, so amended articles of a public company reach the Securities Authority (MAGNA), not the Registrar.

caveats: The Registrar's copy may be out of date for a public company: amendments are filed with the Israel Securities Authority (MAGNA), not the Registrar. The current articles of a listed company are best obtained from ISA/MAGNA or the company's public filings.
UNVERIFIED whether the Registrar practically supplies the founding articles document on request for older/converted public companies; grounded in s.8(1)+s.43 rather than a tested retrieval.
